text: The laparoscopic camera enters through the trocar opening, providing a view of the surgical field.
Three additional trocars are inserted below the chest towards the right side, facilitating access for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon elevates the artery, providing a clearer view of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon creates a tunnel beneath the artery to access the yellow bile duct emerging from it.
The surgeon places clips on the blood vessels feeding the gallbladder, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder and cleans around the yellow tissue covering it, which is part of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon attempts to remove the channel located in the middle of the yellow tissue, which is likely the cystic duct surrounded by adipose tissue.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon continues to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring the area remains dry to prevent contamination. The surgical approach involves creating four small openings under the right occipital spine.
The surgeon creates a slightly larger incision at the bottom of the chest, preparing for further surgical access.
The surgical team inspects the operative field, confirming the absence of bleeding.
The surgeon removes the retractors from the open surgical site and proceeds to close the wound using sutures.
